3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Cover ImageWhen I first decided to explore Data Science, my reasons weren’t entirely my own.
Like many students, I was influenced by what I saw online. Every other video talked about how Data Science was the future, how it offered great career opportunities, and how it was one of the best paths to pursue. Friends were interested in it, social media was full of success stories, and it felt like the obvious choice.
So I started learning.
Over the past few months, I didn’t just watch tutorials I invested time into understanding the field. I learned different concepts, explored multiple Python libraries and modules, and built several Data Science projects as part of my learning journey. Those projects are still available on my GitHub and portfolio because they’re an important part of my growth as a developer.
However, as I progressed, I noticed something important.
I wasn’t enjoying the day-to-day work that defines Data Science. Cleaning datasets, analyzing data, creating visualizations, and training machine learning models didn’t excite me the way I expected.
Instead, I found myself enjoying something completely different.
Whenever I designed a feature, built a web application, debugged a challenging issue, or thought about how different parts of a system fit together, I became deeply engaged. I enjoyed engineering software, solving technical problems, and building products from the ground up.
That realization made me reflect on why I had chosen Data Science in the first place.
The truth is, my decision was influenced by trends, online content, and the excitement surrounding the field. There is absolutely nothing wrong with Data Science it simply wasn’t the right fit for how I naturally think and what I genuinely enjoy building.
So I made a decision.
Instead of constantly switching between different technologies and career paths every few months, I want to commit to one direction and develop deep expertise in it.
From this point forward, my focus will be on strengthening my foundations in Python, software development, computer science, and system design. My goal is to become an engineer who builds software that solves meaningful real-world problems.
This doesn’t mean my time spent learning Data Science was wasted.
Quite the opposite.
It taught me how to learn independently, complete projects, explore unfamiliar technologies, and, most importantly, understand what kind of work truly motivates me. Every project I built during that journey contributed to my growth, and I’m proud to keep them as part of my learning history.
One of the projects I’m now working toward is Truth Engine, alongside improving my portfolio and continuing to build software that reflects the direction I’ve chosen.
If there’s one lesson I’d like to share with anyone who is still deciding their career path, it’s this:
Don’t choose a field because it’s popular. Choose it because you genuinely enjoy the work you’ll be doing every day.
Exploring different areas isn’t a mistake. It’s part of discovering what fits you. What’s important is having the courage to recognize when something isn’t right for you and then committing yourself fully to the path that is.
This isn’t the end of a journey.
It’s the beginning of a more focused one.
Here’s to building software that matters.
